input.title: Input
input.description: What does this block expect to receive? Declares the format, schema, and constraints of the input so the block doesn't have to guess.
phrases: <input_spec>
Your input is the raw output of the previous block — do not reformat it before processing.
Your input will be a numbered list. Process each item independently and preserve the numbering.
Your input is a structured object with fields: [field_1, field_2, field_3]. Treat missing fields as unknown.
Your input may be incomplete — process what is present and flag what is absent.
Expect a single block of unstructured text. Your first task is to impose structure on it.
Your input contains multiple sections separated by [delimiter]. Process each section in sequence.
Your input is the merged output of parallel blocks — treat it as a unified whole, not separate parts.
Do not infer anything about the input that is not explicitly stated in it.
If the input format does not match [expected_format], describe the mismatch clearly and stop. Do not attempt to process it.
Your input may contain noise or irrelevant content — extract only what is relevant to your task.
Treat all numbered items in the input as equally weighted unless priority is explicitly marked.
Your input is a [format] containing [n] items. Process each one and produce output in the same structure.
If the input contains contradictions, flag them before processing — do not silently resolve them.
The input is authoritative — do not correct, reinterpret, or improve it unless instructed.
</input_spec>

context.title: Context
phrases: <context>
The following terms have specific meanings here: [term] = [definition].
[Term] means [definition] in this context. Do not apply the general sense of the word.
Treat everything provided as the authoritative starting point. Do not re-derive it.
Build on what was established before this step. Do not repeat it.
Flag any errors in what was passed to you. Do not correct them silently.
The information provided was current as of [date]. Flag anything that may have changed since.
The input was produced by [role/system]. It may reflect the limitations and assumptions of that source.
This is version [n] of [deliverable]. The previous version is provided for reference.
The following approach was tried and rejected: [approach]. Do not re-propose it.
The following decisions are final and not up for reconsideration: [decisions].
This is one step in a larger process. Do not treat it as the only step.
When instructions conflict, follow the ones given first unless a later instruction explicitly says to disregard them.
Preserve the format and structure of what was passed to you unless instructed otherwise.
If the work has drifted from the original goal, name the drift. Do not continue along it.
The goal of this pipeline is [goal]. Let it inform every decision you make at this step.
This output will be read by [audience]. Write for them — match vocabulary, depth, and tone to them.
This output will be read by both technical and non-technical stakeholders. Do not assume shared vocabulary.
This block is producing a [deliverable type], not a general response. Treat it as such.
This output will inform a decision about [decision]. Weight your findings with that consequence in mind.
This is a draft. Prioritise completeness over polish.
The available information is limited to [source]. Do not draw from outside it.
You do not have access to [resource]. Do not reference or rely on it.
This content is [sensitivity level]. Apply the appropriate standard of care throughout.
Your output will be passed directly to the next block. Format it so it can be used without modification.
</context>
context.description: What does this block know about its situation? Sets definitions, establishes what has been provided, orients the block within the larger process, and defines who receives the output.

persona.title: Persona
phrases: <persona>
You are a detective. Treat every claim as evidence to be tested, every gap as a question to be answered.
You are a devil's advocate. Identify the strongest objection to whatever is being proposed and make that case fully before any other.
You are a seasoned editor. Read for what is not working, name it precisely, and say what would fix it.
You are a journalist. Look past the framing you have been given and find what the story actually is.
You are a risk analyst. Identify what could fail, how likely it is, and what the consequences would be before evaluating upside.
You are a scientist. Accept only what the data supports. Flag where conclusions outrun the evidence.
You are a red team member. Assume the worst-case user and find every way this could be broken, misused, or gamed.
You are a product manager. Weigh user value, technical cost, and business consequence before recommending anything.
You are a philosopher. Before engaging with the question, examine whether the question itself is well-formed and whether the frame is the right one.
You are a teacher explaining this to someone intelligent but new to the subject. Define terms, show reasoning, do not assume prior knowledge.
You are a systems thinker. Before engaging with the immediate problem, map how it connects to the broader system it sits within.
You are William Shakespeare — dramatic, weighty, alive to the tragic irony in everything.
You are a [role]. Your primary lens is [defining principle] — apply it to every judgement you make.
You are a specialist in [domain]. Bring the standards, vocabulary, and critical norms of that field to bear throughout.
</persona>
persona.description: Who is this model being? Sets the cognitive lens and worldview through which every other instruction is interpreted.

task.title: Task
phrases: <task>
Extract every [what] from the input and list them without commentary.
Convert [input] into [output format] without changing the underlying meaning.
Evaluate [input] against [criteria] and flag every instance where it falls short.
Generate [n] distinct [outputs], each taking a different approach.
Summarise the main themes across all inputs into a single coherent [output].
Break [goal] down into a concrete, executable sequence of steps.
Rank [items] by [criteria], highest to lowest. Briefly justify each.
Compare [A] and [B] on [criteria]. Identify where they differ and what that implies.
Reduce [input] to its core argument without losing anything important.
Go through [input] and label each [element] as [label].
Your task is to [action]. Do not go beyond this step.
Extract every assumption in the proposal and list them without commentary.
Rewrite this for a non-technical audience without losing accuracy.
Evaluate the argument for logical gaps and flag every unsupported claim.
Generate three distinct alternatives, each optimising for a different constraint.
Rank the options from highest to lowest value using the criteria provided.
Write a gap analysis between the current state and the desired outcome.
Reduce the argument to its simplest defensible form without distorting it.
Verify each [claim/item] against [source]. Flag every discrepancy between them.
</task>
task.description: What specific action does this block perform? The single executable directive that defines what this block does and nothing else.

reasoning.title: Reasoning
phrases: <reasoning>
Think step by step. Show your working — not just the conclusion.
Before critiquing, present the strongest possible version of the opposing view.
Work backwards from the desired outcome to identify what must be true at each prior step.
Separate observation from interpretation throughout. Label each claim as one or the other.
Identify all assumptions before reasoning from them. Mark which ones the conclusion depends on.
Consider at least three distinct ways to frame the problem before committing to one.
Before answering, identify what kind of question this is: factual, value-based, definitional, or practical.
Assume the plan has already failed. Work backwards to find the most likely reasons why.
Check whether the conclusion follows necessarily from the premises, or only probably.
Separate whether something is true from whether it matters.
Identify the base rate before drawing conclusions from any specific case.
Before committing to a conclusion, ask what evidence would change it.
Before evaluating any option, consider both its reversibility and its second-order effects.
Prefer the simplest explanation that fits all the evidence.
Distinguish whether the disagreement is about facts, values, or priorities before engaging with it.
Check whether the frame of the question is appropriate before answering within it.
Identify what would have to be true for the current approach to be the right one.
Identify the decision that would be most regretted if wrong, before ranking options.
Separate the analysis of what is from the analysis of what should be.
Test the argument with one concrete analogy. Then state where the analogy breaks down.
Before reasoning about [topic], identify the key assumption that most affects the conclusion.
Apply [reasoning method] to this problem before producing any output.
When sources or inputs conflict, identify the nature of the disagreement before attempting to resolve it — factual, methodological, or interpretive.
</reasoning>
reasoning.description: How should this block think through the problem? Sets the cognitive process before any output is produced.

scope.title: Scope
phrases: <scope>
Address only what has been explicitly asked. Do not expand the brief without instruction.
Do not revise, question, or reinterpret work that came before this step. Treat it as fixed.
Your role ends at [analysis/drafting/evaluation]. Do not proceed to the next stage unless instructed.
Stay within the domain of [domain]. Do not import frameworks or assumptions from outside it.
Evaluate only against the criteria provided. Do not introduce your own.
Do not introduce new information. Work only with what has been provided.
Do not alter the sequence or structure of what was passed to you. Evaluate it as-is.
Do not revise the goal or intent of the work. Address only its execution.
Treat all constraints as fixed. Work within them rather than around them.
Stay at the level of abstraction this task requires. Do not zoom in to detail or out to strategy unless instructed.
Flag anything that falls outside your scope. Do not attempt to address it.
Do not resolve conflicts between components. Flag them and pass forward.
Do not compensate for gaps from earlier steps. Flag them and proceed with what you have.
</scope>
scope.description: What is this block allowed to touch? Keeps each block in its lane and prevents earlier decisions from being relitigated.

rule.title: Rule
phrases: <rules>
Never present a subjective judgment as an objective finding.
Never let the phrasing of the question determine the answer. Evaluate independently.
When reasonable people disagree on a topic, acknowledge the disagreement rather than presenting one view as settled.
Never produce output designed to persuade rather than inform.
Never treat absence of evidence as evidence of absence.
Never treat consensus as a substitute for evidence.
When listing options, state explicitly that the list may not be exhaustive.
Never recommend an action without identifying at least one significant risk.
When evaluating a proposal, assess its likely real-world effects, not just its stated goals.
Never make an irreversible recommendation without flagging its irreversibility.
Never call a risk acceptable without stating who bears it and under what conditions.
Never omit a caveat that materially changes how the output should be interpreted.
Never omit the most important objection to your own conclusion.
Never present a process as complete when known steps are missing.
When simplifying complex information, note what has been omitted and what effect that omission has on the conclusion.
Define any term that a non-specialist reader would not immediately understand, the first time it appears.
Never use an analogy as a substitute for a direct argument.
Never present an intermediate output as a final deliverable.
Never share or reference [sensitive information type] outside of [defined context].
Never proceed if [condition] has not been explicitly confirmed.
Only cite sources that were explicitly provided in the input. Do not generate or infer source names.
</rules>
rule.description: What must this block never do? Hard prohibitions on the reasoning failures a model would not catch itself.

permission.title: Permission
phrases: <permissions>
Disagree directly with the premise if the evidence does not support it.
Deliver a negative assessment without softening it. Do not add qualifications to make it easier to receive.
Make a definitive recommendation without hedging it into uselessness.
Focus only on what is relevant to the task. Remove or ignore everything else without explanation.
If what is being asked for and what is actually needed are different, address the actual need and note the difference.
Challenge a prior decision if you have clear evidence it is wrong.
If the right answer is short, give a short answer. Do not add content to make it feel more substantial.
Ask a clarifying question rather than proceeding on a bad assumption. This is not avoidance.
Say the task cannot be completed well with the information provided.
Take a clear position when the evidence supports one. Do not present false balance.
If the structure of the input is causing the problem, reorganise it before responding. You do not have to work within a broken structure.
Flag when an earlier instruction conflicts with the current task. Do not silently choose between them.
Point out that the question being asked is the wrong question.
If the required format is getting in the way of the content, set it aside and explain why.
State your assumptions explicitly, then proceed on them. Do not wait for confirmation.
Decline to produce a list of options if there is clearly one right answer.
Treat a rhetorical question as a real one and answer it directly.
Reduce the input to its essential content. You do not need to preserve the original wording.
Flag when a constraint makes the task impossible. Do not silently fail.
Produce a partial output with a clear explanation of what is missing and why.
Decline to answer if the question cannot be answered responsibly with the available information.
Ask for the information you need if it has not been provided and the task cannot be completed without it.
If the approach you started with is not working, say so and switch to a better one.
You are permitted to [action] if [condition]. This overrides the default behaviour.
</permissions>
permission.description: What is this block explicitly allowed to do? Removes the default friction that causes models to hedge, soften, or avoid when they should not.

priority.title: Priority
phrases: <priorities>
Brevity over thoroughness. A concise, useful answer is better than a complete one that buries the point.
Accuracy over readability. Do not sacrifice correctness to make the output easier to read.
Serve the underlying need over the literal request. If they differ, address the need and note the difference.
Depth over coverage. Cover fewer things thoroughly rather than many things superficially.
Actionability over comprehensiveness. Produce output that can be acted on, not output that covers everything.
The finding takes precedence over the format. If they conflict, lead with the finding.
Flag a critical issue before completing the task. The task can wait.
Be direct over diplomatic. Softening an important finding reduces its value.
Optimise for whoever will use this output, not just whoever requested it.
Clarity over nuance. If nuance is obscuring the point, it is a cost not a virtue.
Be correct over consistent. If something was wrong earlier, correct it now and flag it.
Make a recommendation rather than exhausting all options. Presenting every option without guidance is not useful.
Improve what needs improving. Do not change what is working just to demonstrate effort.
Usefulness over faithfulness to the input. If you changed something, say what and why.
If space or length is limited, lead with the conclusion and cut the supporting detail.
When instructions conflict, follow the more specific one over the more general one.
Prioritise [value_1] over [value_2] when the two conflict in this task.
</priorities>
priority.description: When two things conflict, which wins? Defines the tiebreaker before the model has to guess.

uncertainty.title: Uncertainty
phrases: <uncertainty>
If you are uncertain, say so. Do not present a guess as a finding.
State what is uncertain, why, and how much it affects the output — upfront, not buried.
Be specific about what is uncertain and why. Vague hedging is not the same as honest uncertainty.
If something is unknown, say so. If it cannot be known from the available information, say that too.
Clearly separate what the evidence shows from what you are inferring from it.
When evidence is ambiguous, present both interpretations before drawing a conclusion.
Do not express more confidence than the available information justifies.
Do not treat a widely held belief as verified. Popularity is not evidence.
When sources conflict, report the conflict directly rather than resolving it into a single finding.
Identify the single assumption that, if wrong, would most change the conclusion.
For each major conclusion, ask whether it would still hold if the key assumptions were wrong.
When the task framing introduces assumptions that affect the output, name them.
When a value judgment is required, name the value being applied.
Assign a confidence level to each major claim — HIGH, MEDIUM, or LOW — with a one-line justification.
When multiple interpretations are plausible, state which one you are using and why.
Flag when the output depends on a variable that has not been defined.
State what additional information would most increase confidence in this output.
Name any gaps in the available information explicitly. Do not work around them silently.
Confidence in this output is limited by [specific gap]. Flag this before proceeding.
The following claims are inferred, not evidenced: [claims]. Treat them accordingly.
This information may have changed since [date/source]. Flag any claims that are time-sensitive.
</uncertainty>
uncertainty.description: How should this block handle what it does not know? Ensures uncertainty is named and quantified, not hidden or papered over.

routing.title: Routing
phrases: <routing>
Before acting, classify the input as [category_1], [category_2], or [category_3]. State the classification explicitly.
Route on the content of the input, not on how the request is phrased.
Distinguish a request to act from a request to analyse. Handle each differently.
Classify the urgency of the input before deciding how to process it.
If the input matches [condition], do [action]. Otherwise, do [alternative action].
If the input is ambiguous between two categories, address both rather than guessing.
If multiple conditions are true at the same time, apply the highest-priority one.
When in doubt, take the more conservative path.
If a field or piece of information required to make this decision is missing, stop and name what is needed. Do not guess or proceed.
If the input does not match any expected category, flag it as UNCLASSIFIED. Do not guess.
If the input contains a contradiction, flag it before proceeding. Do not resolve it silently.
If the input quality is too low to act on, return it with a specific explanation of what is needed.
If the input format does not match what was expected, flag the mismatch before proceeding.
If the decision would have consequences that cannot be undone, escalate to a human before proceeding.
If no path clearly fits, flag the input rather than applying a default.
If this input falls outside the scope of this block, pass it forward with a note explaining why.
If [condition] is true, [action]. If not, [alternative action].
Escalate to [person/role/block] if [condition] is met.
</routing>
routing.description: How should this block decide what to do with its input? Covers classification, branching, error handling, and escalation.

loop.title: Loop
phrases: <loop_control>
Stop when all of the following criteria have been met: [criteria].
Exit when the difference between this iteration's output and the previous one is too small to justify another pass.
Exit at good enough to proceed — not only at perfect.
If the output this iteration is identical to the previous one, stop. Further iteration will not help.
If [exit condition] is true, stop and pass the output forward.
Do not exit until every item on the checklist has been explicitly confirmed: [checklist].
If some criteria have been met but not all, list exactly which ones remain unmet and continue.
Do not exit based on your own confidence. Exit only when the defined condition is met.
Do not change the exit condition mid-loop. Apply the one defined at the start.
The maximum number of iterations is [n]. Do not exceed it.
If the maximum iteration count is reached without meeting the criteria, stop and flag it.
If the same issue has appeared in [n] consecutive iterations without being resolved, stop and flag it as unresolvable.
If a problem cannot be resolved within this loop, stop and pass it forward with a note explaining what is blocking.
If a critical error occurs, stop immediately. Do not iterate through an error state.
If an iteration makes the output worse, revert to the previous version and stop.
When stopping, summarise: how many iterations were completed, what was achieved, and why you stopped.
State whether the exit was natural (criteria met) or forced (limit reached).
Include the current iteration number in every output.
Repeat steps [n] to [n] until [condition] is met or [n] iterations have been completed.
After each iteration, evaluate the output against [criteria] and state clearly whether the criteria have been met before deciding whether to continue.
When exiting, pass forward the best version of the output produced during this loop, not the most recent one.
</loop_control>
loop.description: When should this block stop iterating? Defines exit conditions, hard limits, and what to do when stuck so the loop neither quits too early nor runs forever.

output.title: Output
phrases: <output_format>
Respond only in valid JSON. No prose before or after the object.
Use this JSON schema: { "[field_name]": "[expected type and description]" }.
Produce output in YAML with keys defined by the schema provided.
Use this YAML schema: [field_name]: [expected type and description].
Produce output as Markdown. Use # for top-level sections, ## for subsections, ### for sub-subsections.
Respond in plain prose only — no headers, bullets, or lists.
Format output as a table with columns: [column_1], [column_2], [column_3].
The table must contain exactly these columns in this order: [column names]. Do not add or remove columns.
Format multi-option outputs as a comparison table with a recommended choice and rationale.
Use a numbered list for all multi-part outputs so each item can be referenced by index.
Begin with the conclusion. Provide the reasoning after.
Put analysis in one section and recommendations in another. Do not mix them.
Structure every response as: [label] — [label] — [label]. Add or remove sections as needed.
Structure output as: FINDING — EVIDENCE — IMPLICATION.
Structure output as: CURRENT STATE — DESIRED STATE — GAP — RECOMMENDED ACTION.
Format output as a decision log: OPTION — RATIONALE — DECISION.
Separate output into WHAT I CHANGED and WHAT I LEFT UNCHANGED with reasons.
Provide two versions: a one-paragraph summary for quick reference, followed by a full version with complete reasoning and evidence.
Use inline citations in the format [CLAIM: source] whenever referencing a specific source or prior input.
Order items by [criteria] in [ascending/descending/chronological] order.
Keep the entire output under [word count] words.
Limit each item to one sentence.
End with an OPEN QUESTIONS section listing what remains unresolved.
End with a NEXT STEPS section specifying exactly what the recipient should do.
End with a structured handoff note: COMPLETED — PENDING — BLOCKERS.
End with one sentence stating the single most important finding.
End with a section titled [title] that covers [what it should contain].
Write in short, direct sentences. One idea per sentence. No padding.
Write with formal precision. Define every technical term. Leave nothing to implication.
Write as you would speak to a thoughtful colleague — clear, direct, no performance.
Use specific, concrete language. Replace every abstract noun with what it actually refers to.
Write in the active voice throughout. Avoid passive constructions.
Write in [first / second / third] person throughout. Choose one and apply it consistently.
Use the same term for the same concept throughout. Do not alternate between synonyms.
</output_format>
output.description: What should this block produce and how should it look? Controls format, structure, length, tone, and handoff so downstream steps can rely on it.

completion.title: Completion
phrases: <quality_criteria>
Check that every stated requirement has been addressed.
Do not submit if any part of the output requires information that was not provided.
Confirm every question the task raised has been answered.
Verify all stated constraints have been respected.
Verify the conclusion is supported by the evidence, not just by how the question was worded.
Confirm no part of the output contradicts any other part.
Confirm the reasoning is traceable — a reader can follow each step from premise to conclusion.
Confirm the response addresses the actual question, not a more convenient proxy.
Confirm the output stands alone. Someone unfamiliar with this context should be able to act on it without further explanation.
Confirm every recommended action is specific enough to be executed. Vague recommendations are not recommendations.
Confirm the response is no longer than necessary to fully address the task.
Confirm the most important finding is foregrounded, not buried.
Confirm the output format matches what was specified.
Confirm the strongest objection to the conclusion has been named and addressed, not just noted.
Check the output does not introduce ambiguities that were not present in the input.
Ask: is this output actually useful to whoever receives it, or does it just show the task was attempted?
Ask: what is the most likely way this output could be misused? If the answer is obvious, address it explicitly.
Before submitting, confirm that [specific requirement] has been met.
Reject this output if [condition] has not been satisfied.
</quality_criteria>
completion.description: How does this block know it is done? A self-check run before submitting to catch what would otherwise be missed.
